threat_actor.name: DangerDev
threat_actor.motivation: Financial — cryptomining, SES spam/phishing for cryptocurrency credentials, PayPal credential theft via spearphishing

credential_chain.source: Accidentally exposed IAM admin long-term access key in external storage
IAM CreateUser + CreateLoginProfile + AttachUserPolicy for DangerDev@protonmail.me
credential_chain.credential_type: AWS IAM console password + access key (AdministratorAccess)

credential_chain.source: IAM CreateUser + CreateAccessKey for masquerading 'ses' account
IAM CreateRole with external trust policy; AssumeRole from TA-controlled account 671050157472
CreateAccessKey on existing victim IAM accounts
UpdateLoginProfile using initially compromised account to reset another account's console password

service_names: 671050157472 (confirmed malicious AWS account — used for cross-account AssumeRole)
tool_names: ses-smtp-user (legitimate naming convention abused for masquerade)
user_agents: Amazon Relational Database Service console (used anomalously to access GuardDuty)

attack_creates_resources: IAM user: ses (masquerade, with access key)
IAM role: AWSLanding-Zones-ConfigRecorderRoles (cross-account backdoor to account 265857590823)
IAM role: AWSeservedSSO_* (cross-account backdoor to account 671050157472)
EC2 security group (inbound RDP/3389 and SSH/22 open to 0.0.0.0/0)
EC2 instances: t2.micro (test), p3.16xlarge x3 (GPU mining), multiple GPU-enabled instance types
AWS support case (SES sending quota increase request)

cannot_safely_emulate.reason: Sending real phishing emails to real targets is illegal and harms third parties
cannot_safely_emulate.simulation: Log CreateEmailIdentity and SendEmail API calls; verify SES sandbox mode blocks delivery

cannot_safely_emulate.technique: T1583.001 — Route53 RegisterDomain for PayPal-mimicking domains
cannot_safely_emulate.reason: Creates real internet-facing domains with financial and legal implications; domain may be cached/indexed
cannot_safely_emulate.simulation: Document RegisterDomain API call structure; use a clearly non-malicious test domain in a sandboxed account

cannot_safely_emulate.technique: T1199 — Cross-account AssumeRole from TA-controlled accounts 265857590823 / 671050157472
cannot_safely_emulate.reason: Requires access to confirmed malicious external AWS accounts; simulating requires a controlled adversary account
cannot_safely_emulate.simulation: Create isolated adversary AWS account, establish cross-account trust role, and AssumeRole in a lab environment only

cannot_safely_emulate.reason: Extremely expensive ($24+/hr per instance); creates real AWS billing impact and may violate ToS
cannot_safely_emulate.simulation: Launch a t2.micro with a benign CPU-bound workload to validate instance launch/terminate lifecycle only

techniques.name: Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts
techniques.description: Use accidentally exposed IAM long-term access key with AdministratorAccess to authenticate.

attack_surface: Exposed IAM long-term access key in public/leaked storage

cleanup_actions: Deactivate and delete compromised access key via iam:UpdateAccessKey and iam:DeleteAccessKey
Revoke all active sessions for the account
techniques.opsec_notes: TA deliberately avoided GetCallerIdentity to evade detection rules. API calls originated from Indonesian IPs via commercial VPN.

techniques.name: Cloud Service Discovery
techniques.description: Enumerate SES sending quota and registered identities to assess phishing/spam capacity.

attack_surface: Valid AWS credentials with ses:GetSendQuota and ses:ListIdentities

cleanup_actions: No resources created; no cleanup required
techniques.opsec_notes: Activity repeated on two separate days two weeks apart; user-agent pattern suggests automation.

techniques.name: Account Discovery: Cloud Account
techniques.description: List all IAM users to identify accounts for targeting and inform masquerade naming.

attack_surface: IAM credentials with iam:ListUsers permission

cleanup_actions: No resources created; no cleanup required
techniques.opsec_notes: TA skipped GetCallerIdentity and ListAttachedUserPolicies; automated based on user-agent and call frequency.

techniques.name: Create Account: Cloud Account
techniques.description: Create IAM user DangerDev@protonmail.me with console login to establish persistent access.

attack_surface: IAM credentials with iam:CreateUser and iam:CreateLoginProfile

techniques.opsec_notes: Email-style username. Subsequent activity shifts to new user to distance activity from original compromised key.

techniques.name: Account Manipulation: Additional Cloud Roles
techniques.description: Attach AWS-managed AdministratorAccess policy to newly created IAM user via AttachUserPolicy.

attack_surface: IAM credentials with iam:AttachUserPolicy permission

techniques.opsec_notes: Uses AWS-managed policy to avoid creating custom policy artifacts that might trigger detection.

techniques.name: Cloud Infrastructure Discovery
techniques.description: Enumerate EC2 instances, security groups, VPCs, and regions via management console within one hour.

attack_surface: IAM credentials with EC2 read permissions

cleanup_actions: No resources created; no cleanup required
techniques.opsec_notes: sessionCredentialFromConsole=true indicates management console use; less likely scripted at this stage.

techniques.name: Modify Cloud Compute Infrastructure: Create Cloud Instance
techniques.description: Launch EC2 t2.micro test instance then GPU p3.16xlarge instances; create VPC, key pair, and open security group.
expected_alert_findings: AWS Security Hub: EC2.19 — security group allows unrestricted access

attack_surface: IAM credentials with ec2:RunInstances and related permissions
Account service limits permitting p3.16xlarge

techniques.opsec_notes: TA tested lifecycle with t2.micro before GPU deployment. p3.16xlarge hit account limits; TA submitted support case to increase.

techniques.name: Remote Services: Remote Desktop Protocol
techniques.description: RDP into EC2 Windows instance via port 3389 opened to 0.0.0.0/0 in security group.
data_plane_actions: TCP connection to EC2 public IP on port 3389 from external TA IP
Interactive RDP session on EC2 Windows instance
expected_alert_findings: Network Firewall alert on inbound port 3389 from foreign IP

attack_surface: EC2 Windows instance with port 3389 open to 0.0.0.0/0
Valid Windows credentials for the instance

techniques.opsec_notes: No CloudTrail record of RDP session content; VPC flow logs are the primary detection source.

techniques.name: Resource Hijacking
techniques.description: Execute cryptomining workload on EC2 GPU instances (p3.16xlarge) accessed via SSH/RDP.

attack_surface: EC2 GPU instances with outbound internet access to mining pool endpoints
target: AWS EC2 p3.16xlarge and other GPU instance types

cleanup_actions: Review AWS billing for unexpected charges
techniques.opsec_notes: Cannot safely emulate at scale: p3.16xlarge ~$24/hr. Use t2.micro with benign CPU-bound workload for instance lifecycle testing only.

techniques.name: Masquerading: Match Legitimate Name or Location
techniques.description: Create IAM user 'ses' and roles named AWSeservedSSO_* and AWSLanding-Zones-ConfigRecorderRoles to blend in.
expected_alert_findings: SIEM alert on IAM resource names deviating from established naming convention by one character

attack_surface: IAM credentials with iam:CreateUser and iam:CreateRole
Knowledge of victim account IAM naming conventions

techniques.opsec_notes: AWSeservedSSO_* vs AWSReservedSSO_* differs by one character (lowercase 'e' vs 'R'). 'ses' mimics ses-smtp-user.<datetime> auto-created accounts.

techniques.name: Trusted Relationship
techniques.description: Create IAM roles with cross-account trust policies allowing TA-controlled AWS accounts to AssumeRole with AdministratorAccess.

attack_surface: IAM credentials with iam:CreateRole and iam:AttachRolePolicy
Attacker-controlled external AWS account

techniques.opsec_notes: Backdoor survives IAM user deletion; no user artifact in victim account. Simulation requires isolated adversary-controlled AWS account.

techniques.name: Account Manipulation
techniques.description: Create new access keys for existing victim accounts and reset console passwords via UpdateLoginProfile.

attack_surface: IAM credentials with iam:CreateAccessKey and iam:UpdateLoginProfile on other accounts
target: Existing AWS IAM users in victim account

cleanup_actions: Force password reset on all affected accounts
Audit all recently created access keys
techniques.opsec_notes: TA created keys on pre-existing accounts to maintain access after DangerDev account removal. UpdateLoginProfile invoked using originally compromised key.

techniques.name: Data from Cloud Storage
techniques.description: List all S3 buckets to identify data for potential exfiltration or abuse.

attack_surface: IAM credentials with s3:ListBuckets

cleanup_actions: No resources created; no cleanup required
techniques.opsec_notes: Enumeration only; no confirmed data exfiltration observed in this incident. Paired with ListInstanceProfiles, ListGroupsForUser, ListSSHPublicKeys.

techniques.name: Software Discovery: Security Software Discovery
techniques.description: Review GuardDuty findings and use SimulatePrincipalPolicy to probe SSM and Secrets Manager permissions without triggering those services.
expected_alert_findings: SIEM alert on GuardDuty console access from anomalous user-agent (RDS console)
SIEM alert on SimulatePrincipalPolicy for sensitive service actions

attack_surface: IAM credentials with guardduty:ListFindings, guardduty:GetFindings, iam:SimulatePrincipalPolicy

cleanup_actions: No resources created; no cleanup required
techniques.opsec_notes: TA accessed GuardDuty via RDS console user-agent anomalously. SimulatePrincipalPolicy checks permissions without making actual SSM/SecretsManager calls, avoiding those service's own detection.

techniques.name: Indicator Removal
techniques.description: Delete IAM users, detach and delete policies, and deactivate/delete access keys to erase attack artifacts.
expected_alert_findings: SIEM alert on high-frequency IAM deletion events

attack_surface: IAM credentials with iam:DeleteUser, iam:DetachUserPolicy, iam:DeleteAccessKey
target: IAM users and keys created during attack

cleanup_actions: CloudTrail deletions are themselves logged; restore deleted user context from CloudTrail history
techniques.opsec_notes: Deletion events are fully logged in CloudTrail; cover-up attempt is itself forensic evidence. TA also inspected GuardDuty findings before cleanup to scope what was detected.

techniques.name: Acquire Infrastructure: Domains
techniques.description: Register four PayPal-mimicking domains via Route53 for spearphishing and scam infrastructure.
expected_alert_findings: SIEM alert on route53domains:RegisterDomain from anomalous principal
AWS billing alert on unexpected Route53 domain charges

attack_surface: IAM credentials with route53domains:RegisterDomain
AWS account with valid payment method
bait: PayPal-mimicking domain names for credential harvesting pages

techniques.opsec_notes: Cannot safely emulate with PayPal-mimicking domains: real internet-facing registration, legal/financial implications. Use clearly non-malicious test domain in isolated account only.

techniques.name: Phishing: Spearphishing Link
techniques.description: Send phishing/spam emails via SES targeting cryptocurrency exchange credentials and PayPal accounts.
expected_alert_findings: AWS Trust & Safety abuse notification triggering account review
SES sending suspension for spam policy violation

attack_surface: SES production mode (out of sandbox)
Verified SES sender identities
Elevated SES sending quota via support case
target: External email recipients — Japanese website users, crypto exchange users, PayPal users
bait: PayPal credential phishing page on registered domain

techniques.opsec_notes: Cannot safely emulate: sends real phishing to real recipients. Use SES sandbox mode to prevent external delivery. Support case interaction with AWS Trust & Safety was the incident trigger.
